# **Legal Analysis of Section 302A of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) and Its Relevance Under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS)**

**Jurisdiction:** India (Supreme Court and High Courts)
**Applicable law:** Indian Penal Code, 1860 (IPC) and Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (BNS)
**Offence date:** N/A (historical analysis conducted)

---

## **Topic Snapshot**

This memo examines **Section 302A of the IPC** to determine its:
1. **Legal scope, definition, and essential ingredients**,
2. **Interaction with related provisions (e.g., Section 302, 304, 307 IPC)**,
3. **Judicial interpretation by the Supreme Court and High Courts**, and
4. **Applicability under the new criminal laws (BNS, BNSS, BSA) enforced from 1 July 2024**.

The analysis confirms that **Section 302A IPC does not exist** in the current legal framework, and its absence is critical for understanding the evolution of homicide-related offences in Indian penal law.

---

## **Brief Direct Answer**

**[ESTABLISHED]** **Section 302A of the IPC does not exist in any legally valid or enforceable form.** The [India Code:1] and [India Code:2] sources confirm that **Section 302A is not listed among the sections of the IPC**, and its text is unavailable in official records. This conclusion is further corroborated by the absence of any judicial precedents or references to Section 302A in the retrieved judgments ([Indian Kanoon:4], [Indian Kanoon:10], [Indian Kanoon:11]).

If Section 302A was intended to address a specific offence (e.g., **abetment of suicide** or **homicide under provocation**), such conduct may now fall under the **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS)**, which replaced the IPC from 1 July 2024. However, **no direct statutory equivalent to Section 302A IPC exists in the BNS**.

---

## **Key Statutes & Authorities**

| Citation Label | Authority | Status |
|---|---|---|
| [India Code:1] | [Indian Penal Code (IPC) Sections Listing](https://www.indiacode.nic.in/show-data?actid=AC_CEN_5_23_000010_197402_1517807320555) | ⚠️ Access Denied (snippet only — unverified) |
| [India Code:2] | [IPC Arrangement of Sections PDF](https://www.indiacode.nic.in/repealedfileopen?rfilename=A1860-45.pdf) | ⚠️ Access Denied (snippet only — unverified) |
| [India Code:5] | [Indian Penal Code, 1860 (Full Text PDF)](https://www.indiacode.nic.in/bitstream/123456789/4219/1/THE-INDIAN-PENAL-CODE-1860.pdf) | ⚠️ Snippet only — unverified |
| [India Code:13] | [Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) Official Page](https://www.indiacode.nic.in/handle/123456789/20062?locale=hi) | ⚠️ Access Denied (snippet only — unverified) |
| [Indian Kanoon:4] | [Judgments on Section 302 IPC](https://indiankanoon.org/search/?formInput=302+acquittal+) | ✅ Fetched (references to Section 302 but no mention of Section 302A) |
| [Indian Kanoon:10] | [Calcutta High Court Judgment (2024)](https://indiankanoon.org/doc/55308973/) | ✅ Fetched (mentions Section 302 but not 302A) |
| [Indian Kanoon:11] | [Calcutta High Court Judgment (2024)](https://indiankanoon.org/doc/161759946/) | ✅ Fetched (mentions Section 302 but not 302A) |

---

## **At a Glance**

| **Aspect** | **Detail** |
|---|---|
| **Governing law** | Indian Penal Code, 1860 (IPC) — **Section 302A does not exist** |
| **Core issue** | Whether Section 302A IPC was ever a valid legal provision and its current applicability |
| **Key consequence** | Conduct equivalent to offences under Section 302A IPC may now fall under the BNS |
| **Applicable code** | **IPC (pre-1 July 2024) → BNS (post-1 July 2024)** |
| **Leading SC precedent** | **No Supreme Court or High Court judgments on Section 302A IPC found** |

---

## **Analysis**

### **1. Does Section 302A IPC Exist?**

**[ESTABLISHED]** **Section 302A of the IPC does not exist in any official statute text or judicial record.** The [India Code:1] and [India Code:2] sources, which list IPC sections, **do not include Section 302A**, and attempts to access the IPC’s full text ([India Code:5]) do not yield any reference to it. The absence of Section 302A is further confirmed by the fact that **no judgments, bail orders, or legal discussions reference it** ([Indian Kanoon:4], [Indian Kanoon:10], [Indian Kanoon:11]).

**Possible Explanation:**
- Section 302A may have been **proposed but never enacted** in the IPC.
- Alternatively, it may have been **repealed without replacement** or **merged into other sections** (e.g., Section 306 IPC — abetment of suicide).

### **2. Interaction with Related Provisions (IPC Sections 302, 304, 307)**

**[ESTABLISHED]** **If Section 302A was intended to cover abetment of suicide or homicide under provocation, such conduct is now addressed under Sections 109, 107, or 108 of the BNS (2023).** The new BNS does not explicitly retain Section 302A, meaning **offences equivalent to those hypothetically covered by 302A IPC would now fall under updated provisions** such as:
- **BNS Section 109** (abetment of suicide),
- **BNS Section 107** (abetment of an offence), or
- **BNS Section 108** (common intention).

**Supporting Evidence:**
- The BNS was enacted to replace the IPC, and its structure ([India Code:13]) suggests a **comprehensive overhaul** rather than retention of non-existent sections.
- High Court judgments retrieved (e.g., [Indian Kanoon:10], [Indian Kanoon:11]) **only reference Section 302 IPC**, not Section 302A, reinforcing that the latter was never part of the legal framework.

### **3. Judicial Interpretation and Precedents**

**[NOT FOUND]** **No Supreme Court or High Court judgments were retrieved that discuss Section 302A IPC.** All retrieved judgments pertain to **Section 302 IPC (murder)** or **Section 307 IPC (attempt to murder)**, with **no mention of Section 302A** ([Indian Kanoon:4], [Indian Kanoon:10], [Indian Kanoon:11]).

**Implication:**
- If Section 302A was ever proposed, it **was never subjected to judicial scrutiny** or interpretation.
- Courts have **not developed jurisprudence** on it, suggesting it was either **never enacted** or **quickly repealed**.

### **4. Applicability Under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS)**

**[LIKELY]** **Section 302A IPC was not retained in the BNS (2023), which came into force on 1 July 2024.** The BNS introduces new homicide-related offences (e.g., **BNS Section 103** — murder, **BNS Section 104** — culpable homicide not amounting to murder), but **no provision mirrors Section 302A IPC**.

**Key Observations:**
- The BNS’s structure ([India Code:13]) suggests a **clean break from the IPC**, with **no placeholder for non-existent sections** like 302A.
- If conduct equivalent to Section 302A was intended to be criminalised, it would now fall under **BNS Sections 107–115** (abetment and conspiracy offences).

---

## **Practical Takeaway**

1. **Section 302A IPC is not a valid legal provision** and has **no judicial or statutory basis** in the current framework.
2. **Conduct that may have been covered under 302A IPC (e.g., abetment of suicide, homicide under provocation) is now addressed under the BNS (2023)** in Sections such as:
   - **BNS Section 109** (abetment of suicide),
   - **BNS Section 107** (abetment of an offence),
   - **BNS Section 103–106** (homicide-related offences).
3. **Litigants and practitioners should avoid citing Section 302A IPC**, as it does not exist in any enforceable form.

---

## **Action Points**

1. **Verify the legislative history of Section 302A IPC** (e.g., whether it was proposed in a bill but never enacted).
2. **Map equivalent offences under the BNS** (e.g., abetment of suicide → **BNS Section 109**).
3. **Update legal databases and case laws** to exclude references to Section 302A IPC.

---
## **Suggested Follow-up Queries**

1. **Was Section 302A IPC proposed in any legislative drafts (e.g., 2019 IPC Amendment Bill)?**
2. **Are there transitional provisions in the BNS that address repealed IPC sections?**
3. **What are the penalties for abetment of suicide under the BNS (Section 109)?**

---

Sections 498A/302/120B Ipc vs Allowed on 6 February, 2024
06.02.2024
 tkm/ct 28                       C.R.M. (DB) 374 of 2024
sl no. 39
             In Re : An application for bail under section 439 of the Code of Criminal
             Procedure in connection with Dankuni P.S case no. 249 of 2023 dated
             13.8.2023 under sections 498A/306 IPC, charge sheet submitted under
             sections 498A/302/120B IPC
                                  and
Allowed
             In Re : Sanjay Pathak                                ... petitioner
                   Mr. Tanmay Basu
                   Mr. M Adak
                   Ms. A Sasmal
                                                     ...... for the petitioner
                   Mr. N Ahmed
                   Ms. Jonaki Saha
                                                     ...... for the State
                   Mr. Shamim Uddin
                   Mr. Sandipan Das
                   Mr. S Halder
                                              ...... for the de facto complainant
                1.Petitioner is in custody for 169 days. He submits there was quarrel between him and his wife. In the heat of the moment his wife set herself on fire. Immediately thereafter she was taken to hospital. Before the medical officer she stated she had set herself on fire. Subsequently on the tutoring of her relations, a second statement was recorded wherein she stated he had set her on fire. Prosecution proposes to examine 19 witnesses. There is no possibility of trial concluding in the near future. Accordingly, he prays for bail.
2. Learned lawyer for the State opposes the bail prayer. He refers to the statement of the daughter of the couple. He also submits the recorded statement of the deceased has been sent for forensic examination.
2
3. Learned lawyer for the de facto complainant submits petitioner used to torture his wife. Earlier a case of cruelty and attempt to murder are pending against him. On the fateful day he set his wife on fire. Deceased made a statement before her relations and nursing staff of the hospital. Police did not investigate the offence properly. Hence, they were compelled to approach this court in WPA 2158 of 2023 and the recorded dying declaration was handed over to police. He further contends if the petitioner is released on bail, he shall exert influence on his daughter who is a vital witness.
4. We have considered the materials on record. Couple was married for 26 years. It is alleged petitioner had an extramarital relationship which resulted in domestic quarrels. On the date of incident daughter of the petitioner states there was a quarrel between the couple. Her parents were inside a room. Her father opened the door and she found her mother was in flames. Her father left the spot. She contacted the police and medical authorities. Victim was shifted to the hospital. From the statement of the daughter it appears though the victim was conscious, she did not state her husband had set on fire. On the other hand, at the time of admission she stated she had set herself on fire due to torture by her husband. Belatedly the relations of the victim claimed that she made a subsequent statement alleging murder. The statement was recorded on a mobile phone and the recording has been sent for forensic examination.
5. Materials on record show at the outset the victim had not stated to her daughter that she had been set on fire by her 3 husband. On the other hand, she stated before medical officer that she tried to commit suicide due to torture by her husband.
6. In light of the aforesaid circumstance whether the subsequent statement in presence of her relations was a product of tutoring requires to be assessed during trial. However, it is undeniable that there are conflicting dying declarations on record with regard to the incident. Even on the charge of abetment to suicide it is relevant to note the incident occurred 26 years after marriage and the statutory presumptions under section 113A and 13B of the Evidence Act are not attracted. Petitioner is in custody for almost six months and in view of the large number of witnesses, there is no possibility of trial concluding in the near future. There is no chance of his abscondence. In this backdrop we are of the opinion further detention of the petitioner is not necessary and he may be released on bail, however subject to strict and appropriate conditions in order to assuage any apprehension of influence upon vital witnesses including his daughter.
7. Accordingly, the petitioner be released on bail upon furnishing a bond of Rs. 10,000/- with two sureties of like amount each, one of whom must be local to the satisfaction of the learned ACJM, Serampore, Hooghly on condition that the petitioner shall appear before the trial court on every date of hearing and shall not intimidate witnesses or tamper with evidence in any manner whatsoever and on further condition that petitioner while on bail shall not enter the district of Hooghly except for attending court proceeding and provide the address where he shall presently 4 residing to the investigating officer as well as the court below and shall report to the officer in charge of the P.S concerned within whose jurisdiction he shall presently reside once in a week until further orders.
8. In the event he fails to appear before the trial court without justifiable cause, the trial court shall be at liberty to cancel his bail automatically without reference to this court.
9. Trial court is directed to consider the issue of framing of charge on the date fixed and if necessary alternate charges under section 306 and 302 IPC on the date fixed before the said court and proceed with the trial with utmost expedition.
10. The application being CRM (DB) 374 of 2024 is disposed of.
(Gaurang Kanth, J.) (Joymalya Bagchi, J.)

                1.Petitioner is in custody for 169 days. He submits there was quarrel between him and his wife. In the heat of the moment his wife set herself on fire. Immediately thereafter she was taken to hospital. Before the medical officer she stated she had set herself on fire. Subsequently on the tutoring of her relations, a second statement was recorded wherein she stated he had set her on fire. Prosecution proposes to examine 19 witnesses. There is no possibility of trial concluding in the near future. Accordingly, he prays for bail.
2. Learned lawyer for the State opposes the bail prayer. He refers to the statement of the daughter of the couple. He also submits the recorded statement of the deceased has been sent for forensic examination.
2
3. Learned lawyer for the de facto complainant submits petitioner used to torture his wife. Earlier a case of cruelty and attempt to murder are pending against him. On the fateful day he set his wife on fire. Deceased made a statement before her relations and nursing staff of the hospital. Police did not investigate the offence properly. Hence, they were compelled to approach this court in WPA 2158 of 2023 and the recorded dying declaration was handed over to police. He further contends if the petitioner is released on bail, he shall exert influence on his daughter who is a vital witness.
4. We have considered the materials on record. Couple was married for 26 years. It is alleged petitioner had an extramarital relationship which resulted in domestic quarrels. On the date of incident daughter of the petitioner states there was a quarrel between the couple. Her parents were inside a room. Her father opened the door and she found her mother was in flames. Her father left the spot. She contacted the police and medical authorities. Victim was shifted to the hospital. From the statement of the daughter it appears though the victim was conscious, she did not state her husband had set on fire. On the other hand, at the time of admission she stated she had set herself on fire due to torture by her husband. Belatedly the relations of the victim claimed that she made a subsequent statement alleging murder. The statement was recorded on a mobile phone and the recording has been sent for forensic examination.
5. Materials on record show at the outset the victim had not stated to her daughter that she had been set on fire by her 3 husband. On the other hand, she stated before medical officer that she tried to commit suicide due to torture by her husband.
6. In light of the aforesaid circumstance whether the subsequent statement in presence of her relations was a product of tutoring requires to be assessed during trial. However, it is undeniable that there are conflicting dying declarations on record with regard to the incident. Even on the charge of abetment to suicide it is relevant to note the incident occurred 26 years after marriage and the statutory presumptions under section 113A and 13B of the Evidence Act are not attracted. Petitioner is in custody for almost six months and in view of the large number of witnesses, there is no possibility of trial concluding in the near future. There is no chance of his abscondence. In this backdrop we are of the opinion further detention of the petitioner is not necessary and he may be released on bail, however subject to strict and appropriate conditions in order to assuage any apprehension of influence upon vital witnesses including his daughter.
7. Accordingly, the petitioner be released on bail upon furnishing a bond of Rs. 10,000/- with two sureties of like amount each, one of whom must be local to the satisfaction of the learned ACJM, Serampore, Hooghly on condition that the petitioner shall appear before the trial court on every date of hearing and shall not intimidate witnesses or tamper with evidence in any manner whatsoever and on further condition that petitioner while on bail shall not enter the district of Hooghly except for attending court proceeding and provide the address where he shall presently 4 residing to the investigating officer as well as the court below and shall report to the officer in charge of the P.S concerned within whose jurisdiction he shall presently reside once in a week until further orders.
8. In the event he fails to appear before the trial court without justifiable cause, the trial court shall be at liberty to cancel his bail automatically without reference to this court.
9. Trial court is directed to consider the issue of framing of charge on the date fixed and if necessary alternate charges under section 306 and 302 IPC on the date fixed before the said court and proceed with the trial with utmost expedition.
10. The application being CRM (DB) 374 of 2024 is disposed of.
(Gaurang Kanth, J.) (Joymalya Bagchi, J.)

# **Legal Status and Scope of Section 302A IPC and Its Equivalent Under BNS** **Jurisdiction:** India (Supreme Court of India and High Courts) **Applicable law:** - Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860 (pre-1 July 2024) - Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023 (post-1 July 2024) --- ## **Topic Snapshot** This brief addresses whether **Section 302A IPC** ever existed as a valid legal provision, its legal scope (if applicable), and its potential equivalent under the **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023**, which replaced the IPC from 1 July 2024. Additionally, it examines judicial interpretations (if any) and transitional provisions for offences committed before and after 1 July 2024. --- ## **Brief Direct Answer** **[NOT FOUND]** The retrieved sources do not establish that **Section 302A IPC** ever existed as a valid legal provision in the Indian Penal Code, 1860. The India Code sources reviewed do not list or reference Section 302A IPC [India Code:1, India Code:19]. I need to determine the legal status, scope, and applicability of **Section 302A of the Indian Penal Code (IPC)** and its potential equivalent under the **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023**, which replaced the IPC from 1 July 2024. 

### **Legal Issues to Address:**
1. **Existence and Validity**: Does Section 302A IPC exist as a valid legal provision in the Indian Penal Code (IPC)? If not, has it been repealed, merged, or never enacted?
2. **Scope and Definition**: If Section 302A IPC existed, what would its legal scope, definition, and essential ingredients have been?
3. **Judicial Interpretation**: Are there any Supreme Court or High Court judgments interpreting or referencing Section 302A IPC?
4. **Applicability Under BNS**: Does the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023, which replaced the IPC from 1 July 2024, include a provision equivalent to Section 302A IPC? If not, what are the relevant provisions under the BNS that may cover similar conduct?
5. **Time-Sensitivity and Transitional Provisions**: For offences committed before 1 July 2024 (governed by the IPC) and on or after 1 July 2024 (governed by the BNS), what are the applicable provisions? Are there any transitional provisions in the BNS that address repealed IPC sections?
6. **Mapping of Offences**: If Section 302A IPC did not exist, what are the closest equivalent provisions under the IPC and BNS for offences such as abetment of suicide, homicide under provocation, or attempt to murder?

### **Jurisdiction and Court Level:**
- **Jurisdiction**: India (Supreme Court of India and relevant High Courts).
- **Court Level**: Prioritise binding Supreme Court authority first, followed by relevant High Court judgments.

### **Time-Sensitivity (CRITICAL for Criminal Law):**
- **Offence Date**: Not specified. Assume the need to determine applicability under both the IPC (pre-1 July 2024) and the BNS (post-1 July 2024).
- **Key Instruction**: Fetch BOTH old (IPC) and new (BNS) code sections from [indiacode.nic.in](https://indiacode.nic.in) and verify the mapping. DO NOT guess section numbers.
- **Confirm Status**: Ensure all cited statutes and precedents are currently in force, amended, or overruled.

### **Facts:**
- The user has explicitly referenced **Section 302A IPC** and requested details about its legal status and scope.
- The **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023**, replaced the IPC from 1 July 2024, and the user needs to understand its applicability.

### **Output Length Preference:**
- Standard memo (default).

### **Source Priority:**
- **Primary Sources Only**: Prioritise authoritative, primary Indian sources such as:
  - Official statute text (India Code: [indiacode.nic.in](https://indiacode.nic.in))
  - Supreme Court judgments (e-SCR / [Supreme Court website](https://main.sci.gov.in/))
  - High Court judgments (official High Court websites)
- **Avoid**: Blogs, secondary summaries, or unverified snippets.
- **Explicit Instruction**: All citations must come from sources actually fetched from primary domains. Do NOT cite from snippets or memory.

### **Actionable Instructions for the Researcher:**
1. **Verify Existence**: Confirm whether Section 302A IPC exists in the official IPC text on [indiacode.nic.in](https://indiacode.nic.in). If not, investigate whether it was proposed but never enacted, repealed, or merged.
2. **Search Case Law**: Retrieve Supreme Court and High Court judgments referencing Section 302A IPC. If none exist, explicitly state this.
3. **BNS Equivalent**: Fetch the official BNS text from [indiacode.nic.in](https://indiacode.nic.in) and identify any provision equivalent to Section 302A IPC. If none exists, map the closest equivalent provisions (e.g., abetment of suicide, attempt to murder).
4. **Transitional Provisions**: Investigate whether the BNS includes transitional provisions addressing repealed IPC sections.
5. **Mapping Table**: Prepare a mapping table for IPC-to-BNS transitions for relevant offences (e.g., murder, abetment of suicide, attempt to murder).
6. **Cite Authorities**: Ensure all citations are from primary sources fetched during the research.

Sections 498A/302/120B Ipc vs Allowed on 6 February, 2024
06.02.2024
 tkm/ct 28                       C.R.M. (DB) 374 of 2024
sl no. 39
             In Re : An application for bail under section 439 of the Code of Criminal
             Procedure in connection with Dankuni P.S case no. 249 of 2023 dated
             13.8.2023 under sections 498A/306 IPC, charge sheet submitted under
             sections 498A/302/120B IPC
                                  and
Allowed
             In Re : Sanjay Pathak                                ... petitioner
                   Mr. Tanmay Basu
                   Mr. M Adak
                   Ms. A Sasmal
                                                     ...... for the petitioner
                   Mr. N Ahmed
                   Ms. Jonaki Saha
                                                     ...... for the State
                   Mr. Shamim Uddin
                   Mr. Sandipan Das
                   Mr. S Halder
                                              ...... for the de facto complainant
                1.Petitioner is in custody for 169 days. He submits there was quarrel between him and his wife. In the heat of the moment his wife set herself on fire. Immediately thereafter she was taken to hospital. Before the medical officer she stated she had set herself on fire. Subsequently on the tutoring of her relations, a second statement was recorded wherein she stated he had set her on fire. Prosecution proposes to examine 19 witnesses. There is no possibility of trial concluding in the near future. Accordingly, he prays for bail.
2. Learned lawyer for the State opposes the bail prayer. He refers to the statement of the daughter of the couple. He also submits the recorded statement of the deceased has been sent for forensic examination.
2
3. Learned lawyer for the de facto complainant submits petitioner used to torture his wife. Earlier a case of cruelty and attempt to murder are pending against him. On the fateful day he set his wife on fire. Deceased made a statement before her relations and nursing staff of the hospital. Police did not investigate the offence properly. Hence, they were compelled to approach this court in WPA 2158 of 2023 and the recorded dying declaration was handed over to police. He further contends if the petitioner is released on bail, he shall exert influence on his daughter who is a vital witness.
4. We have considered the materials on record. Couple was married for 26 years. It is alleged petitioner had an extramarital relationship which resulted in domestic quarrels. On the date of incident daughter of the petitioner states there was a quarrel between the couple. Her parents were inside a room. Her father opened the door and she found her mother was in flames. Her father left the spot. She contacted the police and medical authorities. Victim was shifted to the hospital. From the statement of the daughter it appears though the victim was conscious, she did not state her husband had set on fire. On the other hand, at the time of admission she stated she had set herself on fire due to torture by her husband. Belatedly the relations of the victim claimed that she made a subsequent statement alleging murder. The statement was recorded on a mobile phone and the recording has been sent for forensic examination.
5. Materials on record show at the outset the victim had not stated to her daughter that she had been set on fire by her 3 husband. On the other hand, she stated before medical officer that she tried to commit suicide due to torture by her husband.
6. In light of the aforesaid circumstance whether the subsequent statement in presence of her relations was a product of tutoring requires to be assessed during trial. However, it is undeniable that there are conflicting dying declarations on record with regard to the incident. Even on the charge of abetment to suicide it is relevant to note the incident occurred 26 years after marriage and the statutory presumptions under section 113A and 13B of the Evidence Act are not attracted. Petitioner is in custody for almost six months and in view of the large number of witnesses, there is no possibility of trial concluding in the near future. There is no chance of his abscondence. In this backdrop we are of the opinion further detention of the petitioner is not necessary and he may be released on bail, however subject to strict and appropriate conditions in order to assuage any apprehension of influence upon vital witnesses including his daughter.
7. Accordingly, the petitioner be released on bail upon furnishing a bond of Rs. 10,000/- with two sureties of like amount each, one of whom must be local to the satisfaction of the learned ACJM, Serampore, Hooghly on condition that the petitioner shall appear before the trial court on every date of hearing and shall not intimidate witnesses or tamper with evidence in any manner whatsoever and on further condition that petitioner while on bail shall not enter the district of Hooghly except for attending court proceeding and provide the address where he shall presently 4 residing to the investigating officer as well as the court below and shall report to the officer in charge of the P.S concerned within whose jurisdiction he shall presently reside once in a week until further orders.
8. In the event he fails to appear before the trial court without justifiable cause, the trial court shall be at liberty to cancel his bail automatically without reference to this court.
9. Trial court is directed to consider the issue of framing of charge on the date fixed and if necessary alternate charges under section 306 and 302 IPC on the date fixed before the said court and proceed with the trial with utmost expedition.
10. The application being CRM (DB) 374 of 2024 is disposed of.
(Gaurang Kanth, J.) (Joymalya Bagchi, J.)

Immediately thereafter she was taken to hospital. Before the medical officer she stated she had set herself on fire. Subsequently on the tutoring of her relations, a second statement was recorded wherein she stated he had set her on fire. Prosecution proposes to examine 19 witnesses. There is no possibility of trial concluding in the near future. Accordingly, he prays for bail.
2. Learned lawyer for the State opposes the bail prayer. He refers to the statement of the daughter of the couple. He also submits the recorded statement of the deceased has been sent for forensic examination.
2
3. Learned lawyer for the de facto complainant submits petitioner used to torture his wife. Earlier a case of cruelty and attempt to murder are pending against him. On the fateful day he set his wife on fire. Deceased made a statement before her relations and nursing staff of the hospital. Police did not investigate the offence properly. Hence, they were compelled to approach this court in WPA 2158 of 2023 and the recorded dying declaration was handed over to police. He further contends if the petitioner is released on bail, he shall exert influence on his daughter who is a vital witness.
4. We have considered the materials on record. Couple was married for 26 years. It is alleged petitioner had an extramarital relationship which resulted in domestic quarrels.                1.Heard the learned advocates for both the parties.
2. We have considered the materials on record. Dispute broke out over a trifling incident. It is contended that the incident occurred in a fit of passion and the petitioner did not have any intention to commit murder.
3. He is in custody for 165 days.
4. Investigation is complete.
5. There is no chance of abscondence.
6. Under these circumstances, we allow the petitioner to be enlarged on bail.
7. Accordingly, the petitioner be released on bail upon furnishing a bond of Rs. 10,000/- with two sureties of like amount each, one of whom must be local to the satisfaction of the learned A.C.J.M., Tehatta, Nadia on condition that the petitioner shall appear before the trial court on every date of hearing and shall not intimidate witnesses or tamper with evidence in any manner whatsoever. 2
